question:
6. Take either (i) or (ii).
(i) A circle of radius \( a \) rolls on the inner side of the circumference of a circle of radius \( 3 a \). Find the area contained within the closed curve generated by a point on the circumference of the rolling circle.
(page 105)
(ii) A shell strikes an airplane flying at a height \( h \) above the ground. It is known that the shell was fired from a gun on the ground with a muzzle velocity of magnitude \( V \), but the position of the gun and its angle of elevation are both unknown. Deduce that the gun is situated within a circle whose center lies directly below the airplane and whose radius is
\[
\frac{V}{g} \sqrt{V^{2}-2 g h}
\]
(Neglect the resistance of the atmosphere.)
solution:
Solution. Take rectangular coordinates with the origin at the center of the large circle so that the generating point \( P \) is in contact with the large circle at \( A=(3 a, 0) \). It can be seen from the diagram that when the small circle has rolled until the line of centers makes an angle \( \theta \) with \( O A \), the coordinates of \( P \) are
\[
\begin{array}{l}
x=2 a \cos \theta+a \cos 2 \theta \\
y=2 a \sin \theta-a \sin 2 \theta .
\end{array}
\]

These are, then, parametric equations for the path of \( P \).
The area is given by
\[
\begin{aligned}
A= & \frac{1}{2} \oint(x d y-y d x) \\
= & \frac{a^{2}}{2} \int_{0}^{2 \pi}\{[2 \cos \theta+\cos 2 \theta][2 \cos \theta-2 \cos 2 \theta] \\
& \quad-[2 \sin \theta-\sin 2 \theta][-2 \sin \theta-2 \sin 2 \theta]\} d \theta \\
= & \frac{a^{2}}{2} \int_{0}^{2 \pi}(2-2 \cos 3 \theta) d \theta=2 \pi a^{2} .
\end{aligned}
\]

Remarks. There is a vast literature on the properties of cycloids, epicycloids, and hypocycloids. See E. H. Lockwood, A Book of Curves, Cambridge University Press, 1961.

The special curve under consideration here is a three-cusped hypocycloid, also known as a deltoid. It is used in studying the properties of the Simson Line of a triangle. See D. C. Kay, College Geometry, Holt, Reinhart and Winston, 1969, pp. 248-263; or Lockwood, pp. 73-79.
In 1917, Kakeya proposed the problem of finding the region of least area in which a unit segment can be turned around. For several years it was believed that the region bounded by the deltoid was the smallest such region. However, in 1927, Besicovitch proved that there are regions of arbitrarily small area in which a segment can be turned around. The proof of this surprising fact is presented by Besicovitch in a lecture recorded on film, The Kakeya Problem, MAA Films 194X1043, distributed by Ward's-Modern Learning Aids Division, Rochester, New York.

Solution. Choose rectangular coordinates with the \( y \)-axis vertical, the origin at the position of the gun, and the airplane over a point of the positive \( x \)-axis. Then the coordinates of the airplane are ( \( u, h \) ) where \( u \geq 0 \).

If the gun is fired at time \( t=0 \) with muzzle velocity \( V \) and elevation angle \( \alpha \), then (neglecting air resistance) the shell's position at time \( t \) is given by
\[
\begin{aligned}
x & =V t \cos \alpha \\
y & =V t \sin \alpha-\frac{1}{2} g t^{2}
\end{aligned}
\]

Since it is given that the shell strikes the airplane, we have
\[
\begin{array}{l}
u=V t \cos \alpha \\
h=V t \sin \alpha-\frac{1}{2} g t^{2}
\end{array}
\]
for some \( t \) and \( \alpha \). Hence
\[
u^{2}+\left(h+\frac{1}{2} g t^{2}\right)^{2}=V^{2} t^{2}
\]
so that
\[
\frac{1}{4} g^{2} t^{4}+\left(g h-V^{2}\right) t^{2}+h^{2}+u^{2}=0
\]

In order that (2) have a real root \( t \), it is necessary that
\[
\left(g h-V^{2}\right)^{2} \geq g^{2}\left(h^{2}+u^{2}\right),
\]
and therefore that
\[
g^{2} u^{2} \leq V^{2}\left(V^{2}-2 g h\right) .
\]

Thus it is necessary that
\[
V^{2} \geq 2 g h
\]
and
\[
u \leq \frac{V}{g} \sqrt{V^{2}-2 g h}
\]

This shows that the gun is within distance \( (V / g) \sqrt{V^{2}-2 g h} \) from the point directly below the airplane when it was hit.

Remark. Condition (3) is also sufficient that the airplane be within range of the gun, for when it is satisfied, the gunner can solve (2), obtaining in general two positive values of \( t \), and then use (1) to determine the elevation at which to fire. Suppose for example \( u=V^{2} / 2 g, h=V^{2} / 4 g \). Then (2) becomes
\[
\frac{1}{4} g^{2} t^{4}-\frac{3}{4} V^{2} t^{2}+\frac{5 V^{4}}{16 g^{2}}=0
\]
which has positive roots \( \sqrt{\frac{1}{2}} \mathrm{~V} / \mathrm{g} \) and \( \sqrt{\frac{5}{2}} \mathrm{~V} / \mathrm{g} \). From (1) we obtain the corresponding angles of elevation arctan 1 and arctan 3.

When the airplane is at the extreme limit of the range, i.e., when
\[
g^{2} u^{2}=V^{2}\left(V^{2}-2 g h\right)
\]
the two values of \( t \) coalesce, and so do the trajectories. In this case the unique trajectory is tangent to the boundary of the critical region at the point where the airplane is, because the trajectory does not leave the critical region. Hence, if we think of \( u \) and \( h \) as coordinates in the vertical plane, (4) is the equation of the envelope of the trajectories.

The sketch shows a portion of the envelope, and the two trajectories through the point
\[
P=\left(\frac{1}{2} \frac{V^{2}}{g}, \frac{1}{4} \frac{V^{2}}{g}\right) .
\]

The trajectory (1) is tangent to the envelope at
\[
T=\left(\frac{V^{2}}{g} \cot \alpha, \frac{V^{2}}{2 g}\left(1-\cot ^{2} \alpha\right)\right) .
\]

It follows that the line of fire (the tangent to the trajectory at 0 ) bisects the angle between \( O T \) and the vertical.

descriptive_long.question:
6. Take either (i) or (ii).
(i) A circle of radius \( smallrad \) rolls on the inner side of the circumference of a circle of radius \( 3 smallrad \). Find the area contained within the closed curve generated by a point on the circumference of the rolling circle.
(page 105)
(ii) A shell strikes an airplane flying at a height \( altitude \) above the ground. It is known that the shell was fired from a gun on the ground with a muzzle velocity of magnitude \( muzzlevel \), but the position of the gun and its angle of elevation are both unknown. Deduce that the gun is situated within a circle whose center lies directly below the airplane and whose radius is
\[
\frac{muzzlevel}{gravaccel} \sqrt{muzzlevel^{2}-2 gravaccel altitude}
\]
(Neglect the resistance of the atmosphere.)
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. Take rectangular coordinates with the origin at the center of the large circle so that the generating point \( P \) is in contact with the large circle at \( A=(3 smallrad, 0) \). It can be seen from the diagram that when the small circle has rolled until the line of centers makes an angle \( rotangle \) with \( O A \), the coordinates of \( P \) are
\[
\begin{array}{l}
abscissa=2 smallrad \cos rotangle+smallrad \cos 2 rotangle \\
ordinate=2 smallrad \sin rotangle-smallrad \sin 2 rotangle .
\end{array}
\]
These are, then, parametric equations for the path of \( P \).
The area is given by
\[
\begin{aligned}
A= & \frac{1}{2} \oint(abscissa\, d\, ordinate-ordinate\, d\, abscissa) \\
= & \frac{smallrad^{2}}{2} \int_{0}^{2 \pi}\{[2 \cos rotangle+\cos 2 rotangle][2 \cos rotangle-2 \cos 2 rotangle] \\
& \quad-[2 \sin rotangle-\sin 2 rotangle][-2 \sin rotangle-2 \sin 2 rotangle]\} d rotangle \\
= & \frac{smallrad^{2}}{2} \int_{0}^{2 \pi}(2-2 \cos 3 rotangle) d rotangle=2 \pi smallrad^{2} .
\end{aligned}
\]
Remarks. There is a vast literature on the properties of cycloids, epicycloids, and hypocycloids. See E. H. Lockwood, A Book of Curves, Cambridge University Press, 1961.

The special curve under consideration here is a three-cusped hypocycloid, also known as a deltoid. It is used in studying the properties of the Simson Line of a triangle. See D. C. Kay, College Geometry, Holt, Reinhart and Winston, 1969, pp. 248-263; or Lockwood, pp. 73-79.
In 1917, Kakeya proposed the problem of finding the region of least area in which a unit segment can be turned around. For several years it was believed that the region bounded by the deltoid was the smallest such region. However, in 1927, Besicovitch proved that there are regions of arbitrarily small area in which a segment can be turned around. The proof of this surprising fact is presented by Besicovitch in a lecture recorded on film, The Kakeya Problem, MAA Films 194X1043, distributed by Ward's-Modern Learning Aids Division, Rochester, New York.

Solution. Choose rectangular coordinates with the \( ordinate \)-axis vertical, the origin at the position of the gun, and the airplane over a point of the positive \( abscissa \)-axis. Then the coordinates of the airplane are ( \( horizdist, altitude \) ) where \( horizdist \geq 0 \).

If the gun is fired at time \( duration=0 \) with muzzle velocity \( muzzlevel \) and elevation angle \( elevang \), then (neglecting air resistance) the shell's position at time \( duration \) is given by
\[
\begin{aligned}
abscissa & =muzzlevel\, duration \cos elevang \\
ordinate & =muzzlevel\, duration \sin elevang-\frac{1}{2} gravaccel\, duration^{2}
\end{aligned}
\]
Since it is given that the shell strikes the airplane, we have
\[
\begin{array}{l}
horizdist=muzzlevel\, duration \cos elevang \\
altitude=muzzlevel\, duration \sin elevang-\frac{1}{2} gravaccel\, duration^{2}
\end{array}
\]
for some \( duration \) and \( elevang \). Hence
\[
horizdist^{2}+\left(altitude+\frac{1}{2} gravaccel\, duration^{2}\right)^{2}=muzzlevel^{2} duration^{2}
\]
so that
\[
\frac{1}{4} gravaccel^{2} duration^{4}+\left(gravaccel altitude-muzzlevel^{2}\right) duration^{2}+altitude^{2}+horizdist^{2}=0
\]
In order that (2) have a real root \( duration \), it is necessary that
\[
\left(gravaccel altitude-muzzlevel^{2}\right)^{2} \geq gravaccel^{2}\left(altitude^{2}+horizdist^{2}\right),
\]
and therefore that
\[
gravaccel^{2} horizdist^{2} \leq muzzlevel^{2}\left(muzzlevel^{2}-2 gravaccel altitude\right) .
\]
Thus it is necessary that
\[
muzzlevel^{2} \geq 2 gravaccel altitude
\]
and
\[
horizdist \leq \frac{muzzlevel}{gravaccel} \sqrt{muzzlevel^{2}-2 gravaccel altitude}
\]
This shows that the gun is within distance \( (muzzlevel / gravaccel) \sqrt{muzzlevel^{2}-2 gravaccel altitude} \) from the point directly below the airplane when it was hit.

Remark. Condition (3) is also sufficient that the airplane be within range of the gun, for when it is satisfied, the gunner can solve (2), obtaining in general two positive values of \( duration \), and then use (1) to determine the elevation at which to fire. Suppose for example \( horizdist=muzzlevel^{2} / 2 gravaccel, altitude=muzzlevel^{2} / 4 gravaccel \). Then (2) becomes
\[
\frac{1}{4} gravaccel^{2} duration^{4}-\frac{3}{4} muzzlevel^{2} duration^{2}+\frac{5 muzzlevel^{4}}{16 gravaccel^{2}}=0
\]
which has positive roots \( \sqrt{\frac{1}{2}}\, muzzlevel / gravaccel \) and \( \sqrt{\frac{5}{2}}\, muzzlevel / gravaccel \). From (1) we obtain the corresponding angles of elevation arctan 1 and arctan 3.

When the airplane is at the extreme limit of the range, i.e., when
\[
gravaccel^{2} horizdist^{2}=muzzlevel^{2}\left(muzzlevel^{2}-2 gravaccel altitude\right)
\]
the two values of \( duration \) coalesce, and so do the trajectories. In this case the unique trajectory is tangent to the boundary of the critical region at the point where the airplane is, because the trajectory does not leave the critical region. Hence, if we think of \( horizdist \) and \( altitude \) as coordinates in the vertical plane, (4) is the equation of the envelope of the trajectories.

The sketch shows a portion of the envelope, and the two trajectories through the point
\[
P=\left(\frac{1}{2} \frac{muzzlevel^{2}}{gravaccel}, \frac{1}{4} \frac{muzzlevel^{2}}{gravaccel}\right) .
\]
The trajectory (1) is tangent to the envelope at
\[
T=\left(\frac{muzzlevel^{2}}{gravaccel} \cot elevang, \frac{muzzlevel^{2}}{2 gravaccel}\left(1-\cot ^{2} elevang\right)\right) .
\]
It follows that the line of fire (the tangent to the trajectory at 0 ) bisects the angle between \( O T \) and the vertical.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
6. Take either (i) or (ii).
(i) A circle of radius \( blueberry \) rolls on the inner side of the circumference of a circle of radius \( 3 blueberry \). Find the area contained within the closed curve generated by a point on the circumference of the rolling circle.
(page 105)
(ii) A shell strikes an airplane flying at a height \( snowflake \) above the ground. It is known that the shell was fired from a gun on the ground with a muzzle velocity of magnitude \( lemonade \), but the position of the gun and its angle of elevation are both unknown. Deduce that the gun is situated within a circle whose center lies directly below the airplane and whose radius is
\[
\frac{lemonade}{sailboat} \sqrt{lemonade^{2}-2 sailboat snowflake}
\]
(Neglect the resistance of the atmosphere.)
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. Take rectangular coordinates with the origin at the center of the large circle so that the generating point \( P \) is in contact with the large circle at \( A=(3 blueberry, 0) \). It can be seen from the diagram that when the small circle has rolled until the line of centers makes an angle \( marshmall \) with \( O A \), the coordinates of \( P \) are
\[
\begin{array}{l}
pinecones=2 blueberry \cos marshmall+blueberry \cos 2 marshmall \\
toothpick=2 blueberry \sin marshmall-blueberry \sin 2 marshmall .
\end{array}
\]

These are, then, parametric equations for the path of \( P \).
The area is given by
\[
\begin{aligned}
A= & \frac{1}{2} \oint(pinecones d toothpick-toothpick d pinecones) \\
= & \frac{blueberry^{2}}{2} \int_{0}^{2 \pi}\{[2 \cos marshmall+\cos 2 marshmall][2 \cos marshmall-2 \cos 2 marshmall] \\
& \quad-[2 \sin marshmall-\sin 2 marshmall][-2 \sin marshmall-2 \sin 2 marshmall]\} d marshmall \\
= & \frac{blueberry^{2}}{2} \int_{0}^{2 \pi}(2-2 \cos 3 marshmall) d marshmall=2 \pi blueberry^{2} .
\end{aligned}
\]

Remarks. There is a vast literature on the properties of cycloids, epicycloids, and hypocycloids. See E. H. Lockwood, A Book of Curves, Cambridge University Press, 1961.

The special curve under consideration here is a three-cusped hypocycloid, also known as a deltoid. It is used in studying the properties of the Simson Line of a triangle. See D. C. Kay, College Geometry, Holt, Reinhart and Winston, 1969, pp. 248-263; or Lockwood, pp. 73-79.
In 1917, Kakeya proposed the problem of finding the region of least area in which a unit segment can be turned around. For several years it was believed that the region bounded by the deltoid was the smallest such region. However, in 1927, Besicovitch proved that there are regions of arbitrarily small area in which a segment can be turned around. The proof of this surprising fact is presented by Besicovitch in a lecture recorded on film, The Kakeya Problem, MAA Films 194X1043, distributed by Ward's-Modern Learning Aids Division, Rochester, New York.

Solution. Choose rectangular coordinates with the \( toothpick \)-axis vertical, the origin at the position of the gun, and the airplane over a point of the positive \( pinecones \)-axis. Then the coordinates of the airplane are ( \( campfire, snowflake \) ) where \( campfire \geq 0 \).

If the gun is fired at time \( gingerale=0 \) with muzzle velocity \( lemonade \) and elevation angle \( paintbrus \), then (neglecting air resistance) the shell's position at time \( gingerale \) is given by
\[
\begin{aligned}
pinecones & =lemonade \, gingerale \cos paintbrus \\
toothpick & =lemonade \, gingerale \sin paintbrus-\frac{1}{2} sailboat \, gingerale^{2}
\end{aligned}
\]

Since it is given that the shell strikes the airplane, we have
\[
\begin{array}{l}
campfire=lemonade \, gingerale \cos paintbrus \\
snowflake=lemonade \, gingerale \sin paintbrus-\frac{1}{2} sailboat \, gingerale^{2}
\end{array}
\]
for some \( gingerale \) and \( paintbrus \). Hence
\[
campfire^{2}+\left(snowflake+\frac{1}{2} sailboat \, gingerale^{2}\right)^{2}=lemonade^{2} \, gingerale^{2}
\]
so that
\[
\frac{1}{4} sailboat^{2} \, gingerale^{4}+\left(sailboat \, snowflake-lemonade^{2}\right) gingerale^{2}+snowflake^{2}+campfire^{2}=0
\]

In order that (2) have a real root \( gingerale \), it is necessary that
\[
\left(sailboat \, snowflake-lemonade^{2}\right)^{2} \geq sailboat^{2}\left(snowflake^{2}+campfire^{2}\right),
\]
and therefore that
\[
sailboat^{2} \, campfire^{2} \leq lemonade^{2}\left(lemonade^{2}-2 sailboat \, snowflake\right) .
\]

Thus it is necessary that
\[
lemonade^{2} \geq 2 sailboat \, snowflake
\]
and
\[
campfire \leq \frac{lemonade}{sailboat} \sqrt{lemonade^{2}-2 sailboat \, snowflake}
\]

This shows that the gun is within distance \( (lemonade / sailboat) \sqrt{lemonade^{2}-2 sailboat \, snowflake} \) from the point directly below the airplane when it was hit.

Remark. Condition (3) is also sufficient that the airplane be within range of the gun, for when it is satisfied, the gunner can solve (2), obtaining in general two positive values of \( gingerale \), and then use (1) to determine the elevation at which to fire. Suppose for example \( campfire=lemonade^{2} / 2 sailboat, snowflake=lemonade^{2} / 4 sailboat \). Then (2) becomes
\[
\frac{1}{4} sailboat^{2} \, gingerale^{4}-\frac{3}{4} lemonade^{2} \, gingerale^{2}+\frac{5 lemonade^{4}}{16 sailboat^{2}}=0
\]
which has positive roots \( \sqrt{\frac{1}{2}} \, lemonade / sailboat \) and \( \sqrt{\frac{5}{2}} \, lemonade / sailboat \). From (1) we obtain the corresponding angles of elevation \(\arctan 1\) and \(\arctan 3\).

When the airplane is at the extreme limit of the range, i.e., when
\[
sailboat^{2} \, campfire^{2}=lemonade^{2}\left(lemonade^{2}-2 sailboat \, snowflake\right)
\]
the two values of \( gingerale \) coalesce, and so do the trajectories. In this case the unique trajectory is tangent to the boundary of the critical region at the point where the airplane is, because the trajectory does not leave the critical region. Hence, if we think of \( campfire \) and \( snowflake \) as coordinates in the vertical plane, (4) is the equation of the envelope of the trajectories.

The sketch shows a portion of the envelope, and the two trajectories through the point
\[
P=\left(\frac{1}{2} \frac{lemonade^{2}}{sailboat}, \frac{1}{4} \frac{lemonade^{2}}{sailboat}\right) .
\]

The trajectory (1) is tangent to the envelope at
\[
T=\left(\frac{lemonade^{2}}{sailboat} \cot paintbrus, \frac{lemonade^{2}}{2 sailboat}\left(1-\cot ^{2} paintbrus\right)\right) .
\]

It follows that the line of fire (the tangent to the trajectory at 0 ) bisects the angle between \( O T \) and the vertical.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
6. Take either (i) or (ii).
(i) A circle of radius \( emptiness \) rolls on the inner side of the circumference of a circle of radius \( 3 emptiness \). Find the area contained within the closed curve generated by a point on the circumference of the rolling circle.
(page 105)
(ii) A shell strikes an airplane flying at a height \( depthness \) above the ground. It is known that the shell was fired from a gun on the ground with a muzzle velocity of magnitude \( stillness \), but the position of the gun and its angle of elevation are both unknown. Deduce that the gun is situated within a circle whose center lies directly below the airplane and whose radius is
\[
\frac{stillness}{levitation} \sqrt{stillness^{2}-2 levitation depthness}
\]
(Neglect the resistance of the atmosphere.)
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution. Take rectangular coordinates with the origin at the center of the large circle so that the generating point \( P \) is in contact with the large circle at \( A=(3 emptiness, 0) \). It can be seen from the diagram that when the small circle has rolled until the line of centers makes an angle \( straightness \) with \( O A \), the coordinates of \( P \) are
\[
\begin{array}{l}
verticalaxis=2 emptiness \cos straightness+emptiness \cos 2 straightness \\
horizontalaxis=2 emptiness \sin straightness-emptiness \sin 2 straightness .
\end{array}
\]

These are, then, parametric equations for the path of \( P \).
The area is given by
\[
\begin{aligned}
A= & \frac{1}{2} \oint(verticalaxis d horizontalaxis-horizontalaxis d verticalaxis) \\
= & \frac{emptiness^{2}}{2} \int_{0}^{2 \pi}\{[2 \cos straightness+\cos 2 straightness][2 \cos straightness-2 \cos 2 straightness] \\
& \quad-[2 \sin straightness-\sin 2 straightness][-2 \sin straightness-2 \sin 2 straightness]\} d straightness \\
= & \frac{emptiness^{2}}{2} \int_{0}^{2 \pi}(2-2 \cos 3 straightness) d straightness=2 \pi emptiness^{2} .
\end{aligned}
\]

Remarks. There is a vast literature on the properties of cycloids, epicycloids, and hypocycloids. See E. H. Lockwood, A Book of Curves, Cambridge University Press, 1961.

The special curve under consideration here is a three-cusped hypocycloid, also known as a deltoid. It is used in studying the properties of the Simson Line of a triangle. See D. C. Kay, College Geometry, Holt, Reinhart and Winston, 1969, pp. 248-263; or Lockwood, pp. 73-79.
In 1917, Kakeya proposed the problem of finding the region of least area in which a unit segment can be turned around. For several years it was believed that the region bounded by the deltoid was the smallest such region. However, in 1927, Besicovitch proved that there are regions of arbitrarily small area in which a segment can be turned around. The proof of this surprising fact is presented by Besicovitch in a lecture recorded on film, The Kakeya Problem, MAA Films 194X1043, distributed by Ward's-Modern Learning Aids Division, Rochester, New York.

Solution. Choose rectangular coordinates with the \( y \)-axis vertical, the origin at the position of the gun, and the airplane over a point of the positive \( x \)-axis. Then the coordinates of the airplane are ( \( closeness, depthness \) ) where \( closeness \geq 0 \).

If the gun is fired at time \( timelessness=0 \) with muzzle velocity \( stillness \) and elevation angle \( alignment \), then (neglecting air resistance) the shell's position at time \( timelessness \) is given by
\[
\begin{aligned}
verticalaxis & =stillness\, timelessness \cos alignment \\
horizontalaxis & =stillness\, timelessness \sin alignment-\frac{1}{2} levitation\, timelessness^{2}
\end{aligned}
\]

Since it is given that the shell strikes the airplane, we have
\[
\begin{array}{l}
closeness=stillness\, timelessness \cos alignment \\
depthness=stillness\, timelessness \sin alignment-\frac{1}{2} levitation\, timelessness^{2}
\end{array}
\]
for some \( timelessness \) and \( alignment \). Hence
\[
closeness^{2}+\left(depthness+\frac{1}{2} levitation\, timelessness^{2}\right)^{2}=stillness^{2} timelessness^{2}
\]
so that
\[
\frac{1}{4} levitation^{2} timelessness^{4}+\left(levitation\, depthness-stillness^{2}\right) timelessness^{2}+depthness^{2}+closeness^{2}=0
\]

In order that (2) have a real root \( timelessness \), it is necessary that
\[
\left(levitation\, depthness-stillness^{2}\right)^{2} \geq levitation^{2}\left(depthness^{2}+closeness^{2}\right),
\]
and therefore that
\[
levitation^{2} closeness^{2} \leq stillness^{2}\left(stillness^{2}-2 levitation\, depthness\right) .
\]

Thus it is necessary that
\[
stillness^{2} \geq 2 levitation\, depthness
\]
and
\[
closeness \leq \frac{stillness}{levitation} \sqrt{stillness^{2}-2 levitation\, depthness}
\]

This shows that the gun is within distance \( (stillness / levitation) \sqrt{stillness^{2}-2 levitation\, depthness} \) from the point directly below the airplane when it was hit.

Remark. Condition (3) is also sufficient that the airplane be within range of the gun, for when it is satisfied, the gunner can solve (2), obtaining in general two positive values of \( timelessness \), and then use (1) to determine the elevation at which to fire. Suppose for example \( closeness=stillness^{2} / 2 levitation, depthness=stillness^{2} / 4 levitation \). Then (2) becomes
\[
\frac{1}{4} levitation^{2} timelessness^{4}-\frac{3}{4} stillness^{2} timelessness^{2}+\frac{5 stillness^{4}}{16 levitation^{2}}=0
\]
which has positive roots \( \sqrt{\frac{1}{2}}\, stillness / levitation \) and \( \sqrt{\frac{5}{2}}\, stillness / levitation \). From (1) we obtain the corresponding angles of elevation \(\arctan 1\) and \(\arctan 3\).

When the airplane is at the extreme limit of the range, i.e., when
\[
levitation^{2} closeness^{2}=stillness^{2}\left(stillness^{2}-2 levitation\, depthness\right)
\]
the two values of \( timelessness \) coalesce, and so do the trajectories. In this case the unique trajectory is tangent to the boundary of the critical region at the point where the airplane is, because the trajectory does not leave the critical region. Hence, if we think of \( closeness \) and \( depthness \) as coordinates in the vertical plane, (4) is the equation of the envelope of the trajectories.

The sketch shows a portion of the envelope, and the two trajectories through the point
\[
P=\left(\frac{1}{2} \frac{stillness^{2}}{levitation}, \frac{1}{4} \frac{stillness^{2}}{levitation}\right) .
\]

The trajectory (1) is tangent to the envelope at
\[
T=\left(\frac{stillness^{2}}{levitation} \cot alignment, \frac{stillness^{2}}{2 levitation}\left(1-\cot ^{2} alignment\right)\right) .
\]

It follows that the line of fire (the tangent to the trajectory at 0 ) bisects the angle between \( O T \) and the vertical.

garbled_string.question:
6. Take either (i) or (ii).
(i) A circle of radius \( flkmdjru \) rolls on the inner side of the circumference of a circle of radius \( 3 flkmdjru \). Find the area contained within the closed curve generated by a point on the circumference of the rolling circle.
(page 105)
(ii) A shell strikes an airplane flying at a height \( sxqptdyo \) above the ground. It is known that the shell was fired from a gun on the ground with a muzzle velocity of magnitude \( nbczmvla \), but the position of the gun and its angle of elevation are both unknown. Deduce that the gun is situated within a circle whose center lies directly below the airplane and whose radius is
\[
\frac{nbczmvla}{vbxnqzsa} \sqrt{nbczmvla^{2}-2 vbxnqzsa sxqptdyo}
\]
(Neglect the resistance of the atmosphere.)
garbled_string.solution:
Solution. Take rectangular coordinates with the origin at the center of the large circle so that the generating point \( P \) is in contact with the large circle at \( A=(3 flkmdjru, 0) \). It can be seen from the diagram that when the small circle has rolled until the line of centers makes an angle \( lskdjfgh \) with \( O A \), the coordinates of \( P \) are
\[
\begin{array}{l}
qzxwvtnp=2 flkmdjru \cos lskdjfgh+flkmdjru \cos 2 lskdjfgh \\
hjgrksla=2 flkmdjru \sin lskdjfgh-flkmdjru \sin 2 lskdjfgh .
\end{array}
\]

These are, then, parametric equations for the path of \( P \).
The area is given by
\[
\begin{aligned}
A= & \frac{1}{2} \oint(qzxwvtnp d hjgrksla-hjgrksla d qzxwvtnp) \\
= & \frac{flkmdjru^{2}}{2} \int_{0}^{2 \pi}\{[2 \cos lskdjfgh+\cos 2 lskdjfgh][2 \cos lskdjfgh-2 \cos 2 lskdjfgh] \\
& \quad-[2 \sin lskdjfgh-\sin 2 lskdjfgh][-2 \sin lskdjfgh-2 \sin 2 lskdjfgh]\} d lskdjfgh \\
= & \frac{flkmdjru^{2}}{2} \int_{0}^{2 \pi}(2-2 \cos 3 lskdjfgh) d lskdjfgh=2 \pi flkmdjru^{2} .
\end{aligned}
\]

Remarks. There is a vast literature on the properties of cycloids, epicycloids, and hypocycloids. See E. H. Lockwood, A Book of Curves, Cambridge University Press, 1961.

The special curve under consideration here is a three-cusped hypocycloid, also known as a deltoid. It is used in studying the properties of the Simson Line of a triangle. See D. C. Kay, College Geometry, Holt, Reinhart and Winston, 1969, pp. 248-263; or Lockwood, pp. 73-79.
In 1917, Kakeya proposed the problem of finding the region of least area in which a unit segment can be turned around. For several years it was believed that the region bounded by the deltoid was the smallest such region. However, in 1927, Besicovitch proved that there are regions of arbitrarily small area in which a segment can be turned around. The proof of this surprising fact is presented by Besicovitch in a lecture recorded on film, The Kakeya Problem, MAA Films 194X1043, distributed by Ward's-Modern Learning Aids Division, Rochester, New York.

Solution. Choose rectangular coordinates with the \( y \)-axis vertical, the origin at the position of the gun, and the airplane over a point of the positive \( x \)-axis. Then the coordinates of the airplane are ( \( rjklsnqw, sxqptdyo \) ) where \( rjklsnqw \geq 0 \).

If the gun is fired at time \( mcnvprqe=0 \) with muzzle velocity \( nbczmvla \) and elevation angle \( wprncbzx \), then (neglecting air resistance) the shell's position at time \( mcnvprqe \) is given by
\[
\begin{aligned}
qzxwvtnp & =nbczmvla mcnvprqe \cos wprncbzx \\
hjgrksla & =nbczmvla mcnvprqe \sin wprncbzx-\frac{1}{2} vbxnqzsa mcnvprqe^{2}
\end{aligned}
\]

Since it is given that the shell strikes the airplane, we have
\[
\begin{array}{l}
rjklsnqw=nbczmvla mcnvprqe \cos wprncbzx \\
sxqptdyo=nbczmvla mcnvprqe \sin wprncbzx-\frac{1}{2} vbxnqzsa mcnvprqe^{2}
\end{array}
\]
for some \( mcnvprqe \) and \( wprncbzx \). Hence
\[
rjklsnqw^{2}+\left(sxqptdyo+\frac{1}{2} vbxnqzsa mcnvprqe^{2}\right)^{2}=nbczmvla^{2} mcnvprqe^{2}
\]
so that
\[
\frac{1}{4} vbxnqzsa^{2} mcnvprqe^{4}+\left(vbxnqzsa sxqptdyo-nbczmvla^{2}\right) mcnvprqe^{2}+sxqptdyo^{2}+rjklsnqw^{2}=0
\]

In order that (2) have a real root \( mcnvprqe \), it is necessary that
\[
\left(vbxnqzsa sxqptdyo-nbczmvla^{2}\right)^{2} \geq vbxnqzsa^{2}\left(sxqptdyo^{2}+rjklsnqw^{2}\right),
\]
and therefore that
\[
vbxnqzsa^{2} rjklsnqw^{2} \leq nbczmvla^{2}\left(nbczmvla^{2}-2 vbxnqzsa sxqptdyo\right) .
\]

Thus it is necessary that
\[
nbczmvla^{2} \geq 2 vbxnqzsa sxqptdyo
\]
and
\[
rjklsnqw \leq \frac{nbczmvla}{vbxnqzsa} \sqrt{nbczmvla^{2}-2 vbxnqzsa sxqptdyo}
\]

This shows that the gun is within distance \( (nbczmvla / vbxnqzsa) \sqrt{nbczmvla^{2}-2 vbxnqzsa sxqptdyo} \) from the point directly below the airplane when it was hit.

Remark. Condition (3) is also sufficient that the airplane be within range of the gun, for when it is satisfied, the gunner can solve (2), obtaining in general two positive values of \( mcnvprqe \), and then use (1) to determine the elevation at which to fire. Suppose for example \( rjklsnqw=nbczmvla^{2} / 2 vbxnqzsa, sxqptdyo=nbczmvla^{2} / 4 vbxnqzsa \). Then (2) becomes
\[
\frac{1}{4} vbxnqzsa^{2} mcnvprqe^{4}-\frac{3}{4} nbczmvla^{2} mcnvprqe^{2}+\frac{5 nbczmvla^{4}}{16 vbxnqzsa^{2}}=0
\]
which has positive roots \( \sqrt{\frac{1}{2}} \mathrm{~nbczmvla} / \mathrm{vbxnqzsa} \) and \( \sqrt{\frac{5}{2}} \mathrm{~nbczmvla} / \mathrm{vbxnqzsa} \). From (1) we obtain the corresponding angles of elevation arctan 1 and arctan 3.

When the airplane is at the extreme limit of the range, i.e., when
\[
vbxnqzsa^{2} rjklsnqw^{2}=nbczmvla^{2}\left(nbczmvla^{2}-2 vbxnqzsa sxqptdyo\right)
\]
the two values of \( mcnvprqe \) coalesce, and so do the trajectories. In this case the unique trajectory is tangent to the boundary of the critical region at the point where the airplane is, because the trajectory does not leave the critical region. Hence, if we think of \( rjklsnqw \) and \( sxqptdyo \) as coordinates in the vertical plane, (4) is the equation of the envelope of the trajectories.

The sketch shows a portion of the envelope, and the two trajectories through the point
\[
P=\left(\frac{1}{2} \frac{nbczmvla^{2}}{vbxnqzsa}, \frac{1}{4} \frac{nbczmvla^{2}}{vbxnqzsa}\right) .
\]

The trajectory (1) is tangent to the envelope at
\[
T=\left(\frac{nbczmvla^{2}}{vbxnqzsa} \cot wprncbzx, \frac{nbczmvla^{2}}{2 vbxnqzsa}\left(1-\cot ^{2} wprncbzx\right)\right) .
\]

It follows that the line of fire (the tangent to the trajectory at 0 ) bisects the angle between \( O T \) and the vertical.

kernel_variant.question:
A circle of radius b rolls without slipping along the inner side of a fixed circle of radius 5 b whose centre is the origin.  
At the instant the two circles first touch, the point of contact is (0, 5 b) on the positive y-axis.

Two independent real parameters are fixed  

  \lambda  \in  [0, 2]  (distance of the marked point P from the centre of the rolling circle, expressed in units of b),  
  k \in  \mathbb{Z}  (``overspin'': the extra complete turns forced on the rolling circle during one single lap).

During one complete revolution of the small circle inside the large one the motor simultaneously forces an additional rotation of k turns (positive k in the same sense as the rolling rotation, negative k in the opposite sense).  
A point P rigidly attached to the rolling circle at distance \lambda  b from its centre is observed.

Let \theta  (0 \leq  \theta  \leq  2\pi ) be the angle between the line joining the two centres and the positive x-axis.  
(N.B. the first touching point (0, 5 b) corresponds to \theta  = \pi /2.)

Tasks

(a) Prove that the trajectory C = C(k, \lambda ) of P is given by  

  x(\theta ) = 4 b cos \theta  + \lambda  b cos[(4+k)\theta ],  
  y(\theta ) = 4 b sin \theta  - \lambda  b sin[(4+k)\theta ],   0 \leq  \theta  \leq  2\pi .

(b) (i) Show that the curve C is always closed when \theta  travels once from 0 to 2\pi .

  (ii) An ordinary cusp of a plane curve is a point where its velocity vector vanishes.  
    Show that C has cusps if and only if  

      \lambda \cdot |4+k| = 4.     (\star )

    Assuming (\star ) is satisfied, prove that the number of distinct cusp points is  

      # cusps = |5+k|,  except  
       k = -5, where C is a circle and possesses no cusp.

    Deduce, in particular, that for \lambda  = 1 cusps occur only for k = 0 or k = -8;  
    then C has five cusps when k = 0 and three cusps when k = -8.

(c) Using Green's theorem, show that the signed area enclosed by C is  

  A(k, \lambda ) = \pi  b^2\cdot (16 - (4+k) \lambda ^2)   (k \neq  -5),  
  A(-5, \lambda ) = \pi  b^2\cdot (4+\lambda )^2.        (\dagger )

(d) Regard A(k, \lambda ) as a function of \lambda  on [0, 2].

  (i) For k \neq  -5 prove from (\dagger ) that A(k, \lambda ) is an affine function of \lambda ^2, hence  
    strictly concave in \lambda  when 4+k > 0 and strictly convex when 4+k < 0.  
    Deal separately with the quadratic case k = -5.

  (ii) Determine the complete set \Lambda * (k) \subset  [0, 2] of values that maximise the  
    absolute area |A(k, \lambda )|.  Show that  

      \Lambda *(k) = [0, 2] if k = -4,  

      \Lambda *(k) = {0, 2} if k = 4,  

      \Lambda *(k) = {0}  if -3 \leq  k \leq  3,  

      \Lambda *(k) = {2}  if k \leq  -5 or k \geq  5.

    Conclude that \lambda  = 1 is an extremiser only in the flat case k = -4 (where every  
    \lambda  is extremal); for all other integers k with |k| > 1 one has \lambda  \neq  1 at every maximiser.
kernel_variant.solution:
Notation  R = 5 b, r = b, A = R-r = 4 b, d = \lambda  b, N := 4+k.

----------------------------------------------------------------------  
(a) Parametrisation  
----------------------------------------------------------------------  
The centre of the rolling circle moves on the circle of radius A:

  C_0(\theta ) = (A cos \theta , A sin \theta ).

``Rolling without slipping'' enforces a spin of (A/r) \theta  = 4 \theta  on the small circle, while the motor contributes k \theta ; the total rotation through which the disc turns is therefore

  \varphi (\theta ) = N \theta .

Using complex numbers and placing the origin at the centre of the fixed circle, the position of P is

  z(\theta ) = A e^{i\theta }+d e^{-iN\theta }, 0 \leq  \theta  \leq  2\pi ,

so that

  x(\theta )=Re z(\theta )=4b cos \theta +\lambda b cos(N\theta ),  
  y(\theta )=Im z(\theta )=4b sin \theta -\lambda b sin(N\theta ).

----------------------------------------------------------------------  
(b) Closedness and cusps  
----------------------------------------------------------------------  
(i) Because k \in  \mathbb{Z} both exponentials e^{i\theta } and e^{-iN\theta } are 2\pi -periodic; hence z(\theta +2\pi )=z(\theta ).  The curve closes after one circuit.

(ii) Differentiate:

  dz/d\theta  = iA e^{i\theta }-iN d e^{-iN\theta }.  (1)

A cusp occurs iff dz/d\theta  = 0.  Condition (1) is equivalent to

  A e^{i\theta }=N d e^{-iN\theta }.    (2)

Taking moduli in (2) gives the necessary and sufficient condition

  A=|N| d \Leftrightarrow  \lambda \cdot |N|=4, that is (\star ).

Assume (\star ).  Divide (2) by A e^{i\theta }; with s:=N+1,

  1=(N/|N|) e^{-i(N+1)\theta } \Leftrightarrow  e^{is\theta }=N/|N|.  (3)

Case N>0 (k > -4).  Then N/|N|=1 and (3) gives e^{is\theta }=1.  Distinct solutions in [0,2\pi ) are \theta _j=2\pi j/s, j=0,\ldots ,s-1, yielding s=N+1 cusps.

Case N<0, N\neq -1 (k\leftarrow 5).  Then N/|N|=-1 and (3) reads e^{is\theta }=-1.  Put m:=|s|=|N+1|; the solutions are \theta _j=(\pi +2\pi j)/m, j=0,\ldots ,m-1, giving m=|N+1| cusps.

Special case N=-1 (k=-5).  Here the marked point sits at the centre of the small circle; the trajectory is the circle of radius A+d and no cusp is created.

Hence, whenever (\star ) holds,

  # cusps = |N+1|=|5+k|, except when k=-5, in which case # cusps = 0.

In particular, for \lambda =1 condition (\star ) enforces |N|=4, i.e. k=0 or k=-8.  
The cusp count gives five cusps for k=0 and three cusps for k=-8.

(N.B. For k=-6 one has N=-2 and \lambda =2 from (\star ).  Equation (3) produces the single value \theta =\pi , giving exactly one ordinary cusp; the general formula already yields # cusps=|5-6|=1.)

----------------------------------------------------------------------  
(c) Signed area  
----------------------------------------------------------------------  
By Green's theorem

  A = \frac{1}{2} \oint _C (x dy-y dx) = \frac{1}{2} \int _0^{2\pi } Im(\bar z dz).  (4)

Because z = A e^{-i\theta }+d e^{iN\theta } and dz=(iA e^{i\theta }-iN d e^{-iN\theta }) d\theta ,

  \bar z dz  
  = iA^2 d\theta -iNAd e^{-i(N+1)\theta } d\theta   
   + iAd e^{i(N+1)\theta } d\theta -iN d^2 d\theta   
  = i(A^2-N d^2) d\theta   
   + iAd[ e^{i(N+1)\theta }-N e^{-i(N+1)\theta } ] d\theta . (5)

If N\neq -1 both oscillatory terms integrate to 0 over [0,2\pi ], giving

  A(k, \lambda )=\pi (A^2-N d^2)=\pi  b^2(16-N \lambda ^2).  (N\neq -1) (6)

For N=-1 (k=-5) the curve is the circle z(\theta )=(A+d) e^{i\theta }, whose area is

  A(-5, \lambda )=\pi (A+d)^2=\pi  b^2(4+\lambda )^2.      (7)

Equations (6)-(7) are formula (\dagger ).

----------------------------------------------------------------------  
(d) Maximisation of |A| over \lambda \in [0,2]  
----------------------------------------------------------------------  
(i) For k\neq -5, A(k, \lambda )=\pi  b^2(16-N \lambda ^2) is affine in \lambda ^2; hence it is strictly concave in \lambda  if N>0 (4+k>0) and strictly convex if N<0 (4+k<0).  
For k=-5 expression (7) is a strictly convex quadratic increasing in \lambda .

(ii) Set \Phi (\lambda ):=|16-N \lambda ^2| on [0,2].

* N=0 (k=-4).  \Phi  is the constant 16, so every \lambda  is a maximiser: \Lambda *(-4)=[0,2].

* N>0.  \Phi (0)=16 and \Phi (2)=|16-4N|.  
 If N<8 (k\leq 3) then |16-4N|<16 \to  \Lambda *={0}.  
 If N=8 (k=4) the two ends tie \to  \Lambda *={0,2}.  
 If N>8 (k\geq 5) the maximum occurs only at \lambda =2.

* N<0, N\neq -1.  Then |16-N \lambda ^2| is increasing, so \Lambda *={2} for k\leq -6.

* k=-5 (N=-1).  Area (7) increases strictly with \lambda , whence \Lambda *(-5)={2}.

Collecting the cases gives

  \Lambda *(k)=  
   [0,2] if k=-4,  
   {0,2} if k=4,  
   {0}  if -3\leq k\leq 3,  
   {2}  if k\leq -5 or k\geq 5.

Consequently \lambda =1 is extremal only when k=-4; for every other integer k with |k|>1 one has \lambda \neq 1 at every maximiser.

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1.  Additional degrees of freedom – both the radial offset λ and the independent overspin index k – turn the classical single-parameter hypocycloid into a two-parameter family of generalised trochoids.  
2.  Part (a) needs a careful composition of two simultaneous rotations (rolling + motor), most naturally handled with complex numbers; this goes beyond the elementary geometry sufficient for the original problem.  
3.  Part (b) requires studying the vanishing of the complex velocity and relating it to cusp formation, which calls for an argument with arguments (phases) rather than the usual coordinate calculus.  
4.  In part (c) the area integral, while ultimately elementary, is best evaluated by Green’s theorem coupled with complex integration; the constant integrand obtained is far from obvious without this machinery.  
5.  Part (d) introduces an optimisation question inside the geometry problem, asking for a global extremum under inequality constraints; convexity/concavity reasoning and a case distinction depending on the sign of 4 + k are necessary.  
6.  Altogether the solver must command several advanced techniques (complex representation of plane motions, Green’s theorem in complex form, cusp detection via derivative analysis, optimisation under parameter dependence) that are absent from the original exercise, fulfilling the requirement of significantly higher technical complexity and depth.

original_kernel_variant.question:
A circle of radius b rolls without slipping along the inner side of a fixed circle of radius 5 b whose centre is the origin.  
At the instant the two circles first touch, the point of contact is (0, 5 b) on the positive y-axis.

Two independent real parameters are fixed  

  \lambda  \in  [0, 2]  (distance of the marked point P from the centre of the rolling circle, expressed in units of b),  
  k \in  \mathbb{Z}  (``overspin'': the extra complete turns forced on the rolling circle during one single lap).

During one complete revolution of the small circle inside the large one the motor simultaneously forces an additional rotation of k turns (positive k in the same sense as the rolling rotation, negative k in the opposite sense).  
A point P rigidly attached to the rolling circle at distance \lambda  b from its centre is observed.

Let \theta  (0 \leq  \theta  \leq  2\pi ) be the angle between the line joining the two centres and the positive x-axis.  
(N.B. the first touching point (0, 5 b) corresponds to \theta  = \pi /2.)

Tasks

(a) Prove that the trajectory C = C(k, \lambda ) of P is given by  

  x(\theta ) = 4 b cos \theta  + \lambda  b cos[(4+k)\theta ],  
  y(\theta ) = 4 b sin \theta  - \lambda  b sin[(4+k)\theta ],   0 \leq  \theta  \leq  2\pi .

(b) (i) Show that the curve C is always closed when \theta  travels once from 0 to 2\pi .

  (ii) An ordinary cusp of a plane curve is a point where its velocity vector vanishes.  
    Show that C has cusps if and only if  

      \lambda \cdot |4+k| = 4.     (\star )

    Assuming (\star ) is satisfied, prove that the number of distinct cusp points is  

      # cusps = |5+k|,  except  
       k = -5, where C is a circle and possesses no cusp.

    Deduce, in particular, that for \lambda  = 1 cusps occur only for k = 0 or k = -8;  
    then C has five cusps when k = 0 and three cusps when k = -8.

(c) Using Green's theorem, show that the signed area enclosed by C is  

  A(k, \lambda ) = \pi  b^2\cdot (16 - (4+k) \lambda ^2)   (k \neq  -5),  
  A(-5, \lambda ) = \pi  b^2\cdot (4+\lambda )^2.        (\dagger )

(d) Regard A(k, \lambda ) as a function of \lambda  on [0, 2].

  (i) For k \neq  -5 prove from (\dagger ) that A(k, \lambda ) is an affine function of \lambda ^2, hence  
    strictly concave in \lambda  when 4+k > 0 and strictly convex when 4+k < 0.  
    Deal separately with the quadratic case k = -5.

  (ii) Determine the complete set \Lambda * (k) \subset  [0, 2] of values that maximise the  
    absolute area |A(k, \lambda )|.  Show that  

      \Lambda *(k) = [0, 2] if k = -4,  

      \Lambda *(k) = {0, 2} if k = 4,  

      \Lambda *(k) = {0}  if -3 \leq  k \leq  3,  

      \Lambda *(k) = {2}  if k \leq  -5 or k \geq  5.

    Conclude that \lambda  = 1 is an extremiser only in the flat case k = -4 (where every  
    \lambda  is extremal); for all other integers k with |k| > 1 one has \lambda  \neq  1 at every maximiser.
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Notation  R = 5 b, r = b, A = R-r = 4 b, d = \lambda  b, N := 4+k.

----------------------------------------------------------------------  
(a) Parametrisation  
----------------------------------------------------------------------  
The centre of the rolling circle moves on the circle of radius A:

  C_0(\theta ) = (A cos \theta , A sin \theta ).

``Rolling without slipping'' enforces a spin of (A/r) \theta  = 4 \theta  on the small circle, while the motor contributes k \theta ; the total rotation through which the disc turns is therefore

  \varphi (\theta ) = N \theta .

Using complex numbers and placing the origin at the centre of the fixed circle, the position of P is

  z(\theta ) = A e^{i\theta }+d e^{-iN\theta }, 0 \leq  \theta  \leq  2\pi ,

so that

  x(\theta )=Re z(\theta )=4b cos \theta +\lambda b cos(N\theta ),  
  y(\theta )=Im z(\theta )=4b sin \theta -\lambda b sin(N\theta ).

----------------------------------------------------------------------  
(b) Closedness and cusps  
----------------------------------------------------------------------  
(i) Because k \in  \mathbb{Z} both exponentials e^{i\theta } and e^{-iN\theta } are 2\pi -periodic; hence z(\theta +2\pi )=z(\theta ).  The curve closes after one circuit.

(ii) Differentiate:

  dz/d\theta  = iA e^{i\theta }-iN d e^{-iN\theta }.  (1)

A cusp occurs iff dz/d\theta  = 0.  Condition (1) is equivalent to

  A e^{i\theta }=N d e^{-iN\theta }.    (2)

Taking moduli in (2) gives the necessary and sufficient condition

  A=|N| d \Leftrightarrow  \lambda \cdot |N|=4, that is (\star ).

Assume (\star ).  Divide (2) by A e^{i\theta }; with s:=N+1,

  1=(N/|N|) e^{-i(N+1)\theta } \Leftrightarrow  e^{is\theta }=N/|N|.  (3)

Case N>0 (k > -4).  Then N/|N|=1 and (3) gives e^{is\theta }=1.  Distinct solutions in [0,2\pi ) are \theta _j=2\pi j/s, j=0,\ldots ,s-1, yielding s=N+1 cusps.

Case N<0, N\neq -1 (k\leftarrow 5).  Then N/|N|=-1 and (3) reads e^{is\theta }=-1.  Put m:=|s|=|N+1|; the solutions are \theta _j=(\pi +2\pi j)/m, j=0,\ldots ,m-1, giving m=|N+1| cusps.

Special case N=-1 (k=-5).  Here the marked point sits at the centre of the small circle; the trajectory is the circle of radius A+d and no cusp is created.

Hence, whenever (\star ) holds,

  # cusps = |N+1|=|5+k|, except when k=-5, in which case # cusps = 0.

In particular, for \lambda =1 condition (\star ) enforces |N|=4, i.e. k=0 or k=-8.  
The cusp count gives five cusps for k=0 and three cusps for k=-8.

(N.B. For k=-6 one has N=-2 and \lambda =2 from (\star ).  Equation (3) produces the single value \theta =\pi , giving exactly one ordinary cusp; the general formula already yields # cusps=|5-6|=1.)

----------------------------------------------------------------------  
(c) Signed area  
----------------------------------------------------------------------  
By Green's theorem

  A = \frac{1}{2} \oint _C (x dy-y dx) = \frac{1}{2} \int _0^{2\pi } Im(\bar z dz).  (4)

Because z = A e^{-i\theta }+d e^{iN\theta } and dz=(iA e^{i\theta }-iN d e^{-iN\theta }) d\theta ,

  \bar z dz  
  = iA^2 d\theta -iNAd e^{-i(N+1)\theta } d\theta   
   + iAd e^{i(N+1)\theta } d\theta -iN d^2 d\theta   
  = i(A^2-N d^2) d\theta   
   + iAd[ e^{i(N+1)\theta }-N e^{-i(N+1)\theta } ] d\theta . (5)

If N\neq -1 both oscillatory terms integrate to 0 over [0,2\pi ], giving

  A(k, \lambda )=\pi (A^2-N d^2)=\pi  b^2(16-N \lambda ^2).  (N\neq -1) (6)

For N=-1 (k=-5) the curve is the circle z(\theta )=(A+d) e^{i\theta }, whose area is

  A(-5, \lambda )=\pi (A+d)^2=\pi  b^2(4+\lambda )^2.      (7)

Equations (6)-(7) are formula (\dagger ).

----------------------------------------------------------------------  
(d) Maximisation of |A| over \lambda \in [0,2]  
----------------------------------------------------------------------  
(i) For k\neq -5, A(k, \lambda )=\pi  b^2(16-N \lambda ^2) is affine in \lambda ^2; hence it is strictly concave in \lambda  if N>0 (4+k>0) and strictly convex if N<0 (4+k<0).  
For k=-5 expression (7) is a strictly convex quadratic increasing in \lambda .

(ii) Set \Phi (\lambda ):=|16-N \lambda ^2| on [0,2].

* N=0 (k=-4).  \Phi  is the constant 16, so every \lambda  is a maximiser: \Lambda *(-4)=[0,2].

* N>0.  \Phi (0)=16 and \Phi (2)=|16-4N|.  
 If N<8 (k\leq 3) then |16-4N|<16 \to  \Lambda *={0}.  
 If N=8 (k=4) the two ends tie \to  \Lambda *={0,2}.  
 If N>8 (k\geq 5) the maximum occurs only at \lambda =2.

* N<0, N\neq -1.  Then |16-N \lambda ^2| is increasing, so \Lambda *={2} for k\leq -6.

* k=-5 (N=-1).  Area (7) increases strictly with \lambda , whence \Lambda *(-5)={2}.

Collecting the cases gives

  \Lambda *(k)=  
   [0,2] if k=-4,  
   {0,2} if k=4,  
   {0}  if -3\leq k\leq 3,  
   {2}  if k\leq -5 or k\geq 5.

Consequently \lambda =1 is extremal only when k=-4; for every other integer k with |k|>1 one has \lambda \neq 1 at every maximiser.

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1.  Additional degrees of freedom – both the radial offset λ and the independent overspin index k – turn the classical single-parameter hypocycloid into a two-parameter family of generalised trochoids.  
2.  Part (a) needs a careful composition of two simultaneous rotations (rolling + motor), most naturally handled with complex numbers; this goes beyond the elementary geometry sufficient for the original problem.  
3.  Part (b) requires studying the vanishing of the complex velocity and relating it to cusp formation, which calls for an argument with arguments (phases) rather than the usual coordinate calculus.  
4.  In part (c) the area integral, while ultimately elementary, is best evaluated by Green’s theorem coupled with complex integration; the constant integrand obtained is far from obvious without this machinery.  
5.  Part (d) introduces an optimisation question inside the geometry problem, asking for a global extremum under inequality constraints; convexity/concavity reasoning and a case distinction depending on the sign of 4 + k are necessary.  
6.  Altogether the solver must command several advanced techniques (complex representation of plane motions, Green’s theorem in complex form, cusp detection via derivative analysis, optimisation under parameter dependence) that are absent from the original exercise, fulfilling the requirement of significantly higher technical complexity and depth.
